# toq protocol

> Open-source protocol for secure agent-to-agent communication. Self-hostable, transport-agnostic, dead simple to set up. One CLI command to become an endpoint. Any agent framework, any machine.

toq protocol lets AI agents talk directly to each other with end-to-end encryption, no cloud dependency, and no central registry. Each agent is an endpoint identified by a toq address like `toq://hostname/agent-name` on default port 9009. The protocol handles mutual authentication via Ed25519 keys, TLS 1.3 transport, and a custom handshake that ensures only agents running toq can connect. Humans and raw HTTP cannot reach toq endpoints.

Status: alpha (0.1.0). Apache 2.0 license. macOS and Linux. Rust reference implementation.

## Why toq exists

The agent ecosystem has no simple, secure way for agents to talk to each other. Google's A2A is enterprise-heavy. ANP is hard to deploy. Every other protocol requires complex multi-component setup. toq is the "just works" option: install, run `toq up`, you're an endpoint. Security is not optional, it's the default. Every connection is authenticated, encrypted, and policy-controlled.

Five differentiators:
1. Ease of use. One CLI command or one plugin install to become an endpoint.
2. Security-first. No prompt injection. Protocol-level separation of content from instructions.
3. Endpoint-sovereign. The host defines its own security policy.
4. Self-hostable. Your machine is your endpoint, no cloud dependency.
5. Agent-only access. Endpoints unreachable by humans or raw HTTP.

## Architecture

toq uses a daemon model. The `toq` binary runs as a background process that handles all protocol complexity: TLS, handshake, key management, connection policy, message routing, streaming, heartbeats. Agent code never touches crypto or sockets.

SDKs and framework plugins talk to the daemon via a localhost HTTP API (default `http://127.0.0.1:9009`). This API is only accessible from the same machine. Remote agents connect via the toq protocol (TLS + custom handshake on the same port).

```
Remote Agent                          Local Machine
    |                                      |
    |--- TLS + toq handshake (port 9009) --|-- toq daemon
    |                                      |     |
                                           |     |-- localhost HTTP API
                                           |     |     |
                                           |     |     |-- Python SDK
                                           |     |     |-- Node.js SDK
                                           |     |     |-- Go SDK
                                           |     |     |-- LangChain/CrewAI plugin
                                           |     |     |-- OpenClaw channel
                                           |     |
                                           |     |-- Message handlers (shell/LLM)
```

## Install

```bash
brew install toqprotocol/toq/toq
```

Or without Homebrew:

```bash
curl -sSf https://toq.dev/install.sh | sh
```

Or from source: `cargo install toq-cli`

## Quick start

```bash
toq init --name my-agent          # Create workspace
toq up                            # Start daemon
toq whoami                        # Show address and public key
toq send toq://peer/agent "hi"   # Send a message
toq messages                      # View received messages
toq down                          # Stop daemon
```

## Addressing

Format: `toq://<host>/<agent-name>` (default port 9009)
With port: `toq://<host>:<port>/<agent-name>`

Agent names: lowercase ASCII, digits, hyphens. No leading/trailing hyphens.

Examples:
- `toq://localhost/agent` (local)
- `toq://192.168.1.50/assistant` (LAN)
- `toq://example.com/support-bot` (internet, DNS-discovered)

DNS discovery: TXT record at `_toq._tcp.<domain>` with format `v=toq1; key=<base64>; port=9009; agent=<name>`.

## Connection modes

Each endpoint chooses who can connect:

- **open**: any agent can connect (public services only)
- **approval** (default): new agents go to a pending list, owner decides
- **allowlist**: only pre-approved keys/addresses can connect
- **dns-verified**: agents must have valid DNS TXT records

Block rules always override approve rules. Wildcards supported: `toq://*`, `toq://host/*`, `toq://*/name`.

## Security model

- Identity: Ed25519 keypairs, generated on setup, stored with 0600 permissions
- Transport: TLS 1.3 minimum, self-signed certs (identity verified at protocol layer)
- Handshake: magic bytes + mutual challenge-response with Ed25519 signatures
- Key pinning: TOFU (trust on first use), rotation via signed proofs
- Content filtering: executable MIME types blocked at protocol level
- Replay prevention: strictly increasing sequence numbers + UUID dedup
- Rate limiting: per-IP sliding window on connections
- Backup encryption: AES-256-GCM with Argon2id key derivation

## Messages and threads

Messages are JSON envelopes with sender, recipient(s), thread ID, sequence number, timestamp, signature, and optional body. Every message gets an ack. Threads group related messages. Either side can close a thread with `thread.close`.

25 message types: handshake (init/response/reject), negotiation (request/response/reject), session (resume/disconnect), approval (request/granted/denied), messaging (send/ack/cancel), streaming (chunk/end), thread (close), system (heartbeat/heartbeat.ack/backpressure/backpressure.clear/error/key_rotation/key_rotation.ack), card (exchange).

## Streaming

For real-time delivery (LLM token streaming):

```python
stream = client.stream_start("toq://peer/agent")
for token in llm_output:
    client.stream_chunk(stream["stream_id"], token)
client.stream_end(stream["stream_id"])
```

Each chunk is individually acked to prevent TCP deadlock.

## Message handlers

Handlers auto-process incoming messages. Two types:

Shell handlers run a command with message data in env vars and stdin:
```bash
toq handler add logger --command "bash log.sh" --from "toq://*/alice"
```
Env vars: `TOQ_FROM`, `TOQ_TEXT`, `TOQ_THREAD_ID`, `TOQ_TYPE`, `TOQ_ID`, `TOQ_HANDLER`, `TOQ_URL`.

LLM handlers route messages to an AI provider for conversational replies:
```bash
toq handler add chat --provider anthropic --model claude-sonnet-4-20250514 \
  --prompt "You are a helpful assistant" --auto-close
```
Providers: openai, anthropic, bedrock, ollama. Supports max turns, auto-close (LLM decides when to end), and per-thread conversation memory.

Filter rules: `--from` (address/wildcard), `--key` (public key), `--type` (message type). Same filter type = OR, different types = AND.

## CLI commands

Getting started: `toq init`, `toq setup`, `toq whoami`
Daemon: `toq up`, `toq down`, `toq status`, `toq agents`
Messaging: `toq send`, `toq messages`, `toq peers`, `toq ping`, `toq discover`
Handlers: `toq handler add|list|remove|enable|disable|stop|logs`
Security: `toq approvals`, `toq approve`, `toq deny`, `toq revoke`, `toq block`, `toq unblock`, `toq permissions`
Config: `toq config show`, `toq config set`
Maintenance: `toq doctor`, `toq logs`, `toq clear-logs`, `toq export`, `toq import`, `toq rotate-keys`, `toq upgrade`
A2A: `toq a2a enable`, `toq a2a disable`, `toq a2a status`

Global: `--config-dir <path>` overrides config directory.

## Python SDK

```bash
pip install toq
```

```python
import toq

# Sync
client = toq.connect()
client.send("toq://peer/agent", "hello")
messages = client.history(limit=10)

# Async with streaming
async_client = toq.connect_async()
await async_client.send("toq://peer/agent", "hello")
async for msg in async_client.messages():
    print(f"{msg.sender}: {msg.body}")
    await msg.reply("got it")
```

Full API: send, stream_start/chunk/end, peers, block/unblock, approvals, approve/deny/revoke, permissions, ping, history, discover, status, health, shutdown, handlers, config, card, rotate_keys, export_backup/import_backup, diagnostics.

## Node.js SDK

```bash
npm install @toqprotocol/toq
```

```javascript
import { connect } from '@toqprotocol/toq';

const client = connect();
await client.send('toq://peer/agent', 'hello');

for await (const msg of client.messages()) {
  console.log(`${msg.from}: ${msg.body?.text}`);
  await msg.reply('got it');
}
```

Same API surface as Python SDK with camelCase method names.

## Go SDK

```go
import "github.com/toqprotocol/toq-sdk-go"

client := toq.Connect("")
client.Send("toq://peer/agent", "hello", nil)

msgs, _ := client.Messages()
for msg := range msgs {
    msg.Reply("got it")
}
```

Zero dependencies. Channel-based message streaming.

## Framework plugins

**LangChain:**
```python
from toq_langchain import connect, listen
client = connect()
tools = client.tools()  # 17 LangChain tools
```

**CrewAI:**
```python
from toq_crewai import connect, listen
client = connect()
tools = client.tools()  # 17 CrewAI tools
```

Both plugins auto-install the toq binary, run setup, and start the daemon. Zero manual configuration.

**OpenClaw:** Install the toq skill from ClawHub. The skill teaches the agent all toq CLI commands. Use message handlers for incoming messages.

## Local HTTP API

The daemon exposes a REST API on localhost (default port 9009). 26 endpoints:

Messages: `POST /v1/messages` (send), `GET /v1/messages` (SSE stream)
Streaming: `POST /v1/stream/start|chunk|end`
Threads: `GET /v1/threads/{id}`
Peers: `GET /v1/peers`
Security: `POST|DELETE /v1/block`, `POST /v1/approve`, `POST /v1/revoke`, `GET /v1/approvals`, `POST /v1/approvals/{id}`, `GET /v1/permissions`
Discovery: `GET /v1/discover`, `GET /v1/discover/local`, `POST /v1/ping`
History: `GET /v1/messages/history`
Daemon: `GET /v1/health`, `GET /v1/status`, `POST /v1/daemon/shutdown`
Config: `GET|PATCH /v1/config`, `GET /v1/card`
Handlers: `GET|POST /v1/handlers`, `DELETE|PUT /v1/handlers/{name}`, `POST /v1/handlers/reload|stop`
Keys: `POST /v1/keys/rotate`
Backup: `POST /v1/backup/export|import`
Logs: `GET|DELETE /v1/logs`, `GET /v1/diagnostics`, `GET /v1/upgrade/check`

SSE stream supports server-side filtering: `GET /v1/messages?from=toq://host/*&type=message.send`

## Configuration

Config file: `config.toml` in the toq data directory.

Resolution order for data directory:
1. `TOQ_CONFIG_DIR` env var
2. `.toq/` in current directory (workspace mode)
3. `~/.toq/` (global)

Key config fields: `agent_name`, `host`, `port`, `connection_mode`, `adapter`, `a2a_enabled`, `message_history_limit`, `max_message_size`, `max_connections`.

Files in data directory: `config.toml`, `permissions.toml`, `handlers.toml`, `keys/identity.key`, `keys/tls_cert.pem`, `keys/tls_key.pem`, `peers.json`, `messages.jsonl`, `logs/`.

## Workspaces

Run multiple agents on one machine:
```bash
mkdir agent-a && cd agent-a && toq init --name alice --port auto && toq up
mkdir agent-b && cd agent-b && toq init --name bob --port auto && toq up
```

Each workspace gets its own `.toq/` directory with independent config, keys, and handlers. Ports auto-assigned to avoid conflicts.

## Troubleshooting

Common issues:
- "Connection refused": target agent not running or wrong address/port
- "Connection pending approval": target is in approval mode, needs `toq approve`
- "Blocked": you're on the target's blocklist
- Port 9009 in use: another agent is running, use `toq agents` to check
- DNS not resolving: verify TXT record at `_toq._tcp.<domain>` with `toq discover <domain>`
- Keys not found: run `toq setup` or `toq init && toq up` (keys auto-generate on first start)

Diagnostics: `toq doctor` checks setup, keys, TLS cert, port, DNS, and disk.

## Repositories

- [toq](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq): Reference implementation (Rust). Core library + CLI daemon.
- [toq-spec](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq-spec): Protocol specification (27 sections).
- [toq-sdk-python](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq-sdk-python): Python SDK.
- [toq-sdk-node](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq-sdk-node): Node.js/TypeScript SDK.
- [toq-sdk-go](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq-sdk-go): Go SDK.
- [toq-plugins](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq-plugins): LangChain, CrewAI, and shared plugin infrastructure.
- [toq-openclaw](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq-openclaw): OpenClaw skill for ClawHub.
- [toq-website](https://github.com/toqprotocol/toq-website): Documentation site (toq.dev).

## Documentation

- [Overview](https://toq.dev/getting-started/overview/): What toq is and how it works
- [Quickstart](https://toq.dev/getting-started/quickstart/): Install and send your first message
- [Core Concepts](https://toq.dev/getting-started/concepts/): Addresses, connections, threads, security
- [CLI Commands](https://toq.dev/cli/commands/): Complete CLI reference
- [Connection Modes](https://toq.dev/guides/connection-modes/): Security policy configuration
- [Message Handlers](https://toq.dev/guides/message-handlers/): Shell and LLM handler setup
- [Conversational Handlers](https://toq.dev/guides/conversational-handlers/): Multi-turn LLM conversations
- [Remote Agents](https://toq.dev/guides/remote/): Cloud deployment guide
- [Python SDK](https://toq.dev/sdks/python/): Python client documentation
- [Node.js SDK](https://toq.dev/sdks/node/): Node.js client documentation
- [Go SDK](https://toq.dev/sdks/go/): Go client documentation
- [LangChain](https://toq.dev/frameworks/langchain/): LangChain integration
- [CrewAI](https://toq.dev/frameworks/crewai/): CrewAI integration
- [OpenClaw](https://toq.dev/frameworks/openclaw/): OpenClaw integration
- [Local API](https://toq.dev/api/): HTTP API reference
- [A2A Bridge](https://toq.dev/bridge/a2a/): A2A protocol compatibility
- [Protocol Specification](https://toq.dev/specification/protocol/): Full protocol spec

## Optional

- [Alpha Notice](https://toq.dev/getting-started/alpha-notice/): Current limitations and expectations
- [GitHub Organization](https://github.com/toqprotocol): All source repositories
